eli3: The derivative measures instantaneous rate of change. If position is f(t), then f'(t) is velocity — how fast position changes at exactly moment t. Geometrically, it's the slope of the tangent line touching the curve at that point.
The gradient generalizes derivatives to multiple dimensions. For f(x,y), the gradient ∇f = [∂f/∂x, ∂f/∂y] points in the direction of steepest ascent. Its magnitude tells you how steep that ascent is.

Think of the gradient as a compass on a hilly terrain. It always points uphill, and the arrow length tells you how steep. Following the negative gradient (downhill) is the core idea behind gradient descent optimization.

whenWhy.title: Optimization and gradient descent
whenWhy.body: To minimize a function, move in the direction of -∇f. This is gradient descent. The gradient tells us locally which direction decreases the function fastest. Learning rate controls step size.

whenWhy.title: Sensitivity analysis
whenWhy.body: ∂f/∂xᵢ tells you how much f changes when you perturb xᵢ slightly. Used in economics (marginal cost), engineering (design sensitivity), and ML (feature importance via gradients).

whenWhy.title: Backpropagation in neural networks
whenWhy.body: Gradients flow backward through the computation graph via the chain rule. ∂L/∂W tells us how to update weights to reduce loss. Automatic differentiation computes these efficiently.

whenWhy.title: Physics and dynamics
whenWhy.body: Velocity is derivative of position; acceleration is derivative of velocity. Force is gradient of potential energy. Conservation laws often arise from symmetries and derivatives (Noether's theorem).

deepDiveIntro: For a function from one real number to another, the derivative at a point is the limit of the slope of secant lines as the two points approach each other — the instantaneous slope of the tangent. For a function from many dimensions to one number, the gradient is the vector of all partial derivatives, pointing in the direction of steepest increase.

The directional derivative measures how fast a function changes as you move in a particular direction. It equals the dot product of the gradient with a unit vector in that direction. The direction that maximizes this rate of change is exactly the gradient direction, and the maximum rate equals the gradient's magnitude. The gradient is always perpendicular to the level sets of the function.

deepDive.heading: Definition and Interpretation
deepDive.body: The derivative captures the local rate of change of a one-variable function. You can think of it as the slope of the tangent line or as the best linear approximation near a point. A function may be continuous without being differentiable, so smooth-looking behavior is not enough by itself.

deepDive.heading: Gradient Vector
deepDive.body: For a function of many variables, the gradient gathers the slope in each coordinate direction into one vector. That vector points toward the direction of fastest increase, and its length tells you how steep that best uphill direction is. Points where the gradient becomes zero are natural candidates for minima, maxima, or saddle points.

deepDive.heading: Jacobian Matrix
deepDive.body: When a function produces several outputs, the Jacobian records how each output responds to each input. It is the matrix form of the best local linear approximation to the function near a point. This is the object that makes the chain rule work naturally for vector-valued functions.

deepDive.heading: Hessian Matrix
deepDive.body: The Hessian describes second-order behavior, so it tells you how curvature changes near a point. Around a critical point, its sign pattern helps distinguish bowls, hills, and saddles. In optimization, it explains why some directions feel steep while others feel flat.

deepDive.heading: Subgradients
deepDive.body: Some useful functions have corners, so an ordinary derivative does not exist everywhere. For convex functions, subgradients provide a replacement that still supports optimization and optimality conditions. Instead of one exact slope, you allow any supporting slope that stays below the function everywhere else.

takeaways: Derivative f'(x) = instantaneous rate of change = slope of tangent.
Gradient ∇f points in direction of steepest ascent with magnitude equal to rate.
Jacobian Jf contains all first partials for vector-valued functions.
Hessian ∇²f contains second partials; determines convexity and curvature.
Subgradients extend derivatives to non-differentiable convex functions.

edgeCases.q: What if the derivative doesn't exist?
edgeCases.a: Functions with corners (|x| at 0), cusps, vertical tangents, or discontinuities lack derivatives at those points. We use subgradients for convex functions, or consider one-sided derivatives. In deep learning, ReLU's non-differentiability at 0 is handled by convention (usually returning 0 or 1).

edgeCases.q: When does gradient descent get stuck?
edgeCases.a: At critical points where ∇f = 0: local minima, local maxima, and saddle points. Saddle points are common in high-dimensional non-convex optimization (e.g., neural networks). Second-order methods or noise can help escape.

edgeCases.q: What's the difference between ∇f(x)ᵀv and D_vf(x)?
edgeCases.a: They're the same: the directional derivative in direction v. If v is a unit vector, this gives the rate of change per unit distance in that direction. The maximum over all unit vectors v is ||∇f(x)||, achieved when v = ∇f(x)/||∇f(x)||.
